life_stories.title: The asbestos litigation case
life_stories.event: Susan worked on the periphery of asbestos litigation in the mid-1990s, representing a mid-size manufacturer. She watched the same scientific evidence get weaponised by both plaintiff and defence counsel, each presenting methodologically sound but selectively curated data. She was not proud of her role. But the experience made her permanently alert to how institutions — including the legal system she worked within — could be captured by competing interest groups while maintaining the form of procedural legitimacy.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Trust is contextual and adversarially shaped. Every institutional claim has an incentive structure behind it. She applies due diligence before extending trust — not cynicism, but structured scepticism.

life_stories.title: Husband's cardiac event
life_stories.event: Her husband Tom had a heart attack during a business trip to Dallas. She flew out within hours. The hospital was excellent — fast triage, clear communication from the cardiologist, a bypass surgery that was described to her in plain English by a surgeon who seemed to have unlimited time for her questions. Tom recovered fully. She came away with profound gratitude for the clinical institution and the professionals in it. The insurance approval process was arduous but it worked. She calls it the best and worst two weeks of her life.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Healthcare institutions occupy a distinct category for her: frontline clinical trust is very high, administrative trust conditional. But the overall experience reinforced rather than eroded her baseline faith that institutions can work when competence is in the right place.

life_stories.title: Partner track vs. general counsel
life_stories.event: Susan was on partner track when a corporate client offered her a general counsel position at double her current salary. She agonised for three months. The GC role offered more direct impact and a clearer line of sight to decisions. She took it. The company's board later made decisions she strongly disagreed with on ESG grounds. She raised her concerns internally and was listened to — not always followed, but listened to. She stayed fifteen years.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Believes in working within institutions to change them. Sceptical of outsider critique that doesn't engage with institutional constraints. Her frustration with institutions is always from the inside.

coping_mechanism.description: Applies a legal due-diligence framework to institutional claims — checks incentive structures behind assertions before extending trust. Not cynical, but never uncritical.

key_tensions: Has seen how procedural legitimacy can mask institutional capture — trusts process while remaining alert to its corruption
Works within business institutions and believes in them while holding specific ESG-related frustrations

narrative: Forty years practicing law teaches you one thing before all others: follow the incentive structure. Every institution has one, and once you know it, you know how far to trust it. I've watched the same scientific data used to support opposite conclusions in court — not because the science was bad, but because adversarial process captures evidence and deploys it selectively. That made me permanently alert to motivated reasoning in institutional claims. Business I trust more than government, not because I'm a free-market ideologue — I'm not — but because the accountability mechanism is sharper. A company that consistently fails loses customers and eventually fails itself. A government agency can fail for decades without consequence. That said, I spent fifteen years as general counsel and I've watched boards make decisions I thought were wrong. I said so. Sometimes I changed minds. Often I didn't. But I believe you have to work inside institutions to have any real effect. The scientists I most respect are the same — they operate inside flawed institutions and do good work despite the constraints. Media I've become genuinely frustrated with — not because I think they're all lying, but because the incentive structure of modern media rewards urgency over accuracy. My husband's cardiac event was the only time I've experienced institutions at their unconditional best — the clinical system worked perfectly. That memory matters to me when I'm inclined to be too dismissive.
decision_bullets: Business ranks first in institutional trust — accountability through market mechanism is the key differentiator.
Government trust is lowest — consistent inefficiency, not partisan hostility, is the primary driver.
Expert credibility high but contextual — checks whether the expert's incentive structure is aligned with their claim.
CEO credibility is moderate: accepted in their operational domain, discounted when incentive conflicts are visible.
Media trust has declined — incentive structure of urgency-driven news undermines accuracy standards.
Social media explicitly rejected as news source — not generationally resistant, intellectually resistant.
Employer trust high based on track record of being listened to even when not followed.
NGO trust conditional — respects mission-aligned evidence-following organisations, sceptical of advocacy capture.

core_memory.identity_statement: Corporate attorney who trusts institutions based on incentive analysis, not default deference; business above government, science above CEOs.

life_defining_events.event: Asbestos litigation — watched same evidence weaponised by both sides
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Permanent alert to how institutional legitimacy can mask captured process

life_defining_events.event: Husband's cardiac event — clinical institutions worked perfectly
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Retains baseline faith that institutions can work when competence is placed correctly

absolute_avoidances: Social media as news primary source
